ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for pandan kaya cake; mapped to the closest parent class 'cake food product' (FOODON_03315091) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pandan kaya cake; A00LC (Cakes and pastries) is the closest defensible category-level parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pandan kaya cake or a sufficiently close analogue; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pandan kaya cake in Singapore is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one small jar or a generous individual portion as sold by local bakeries and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: Pandan Kaya Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95 for an exact named match. Cholesterol (100 mg/100g), saturated fat (12.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.07 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.33 mg/100g) were originally attributed to a delivery-platform source in the input record; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18133, Cake, sponge; and coconut cream component data) and are retained as plausible estimates consistent with the high coconut-milk content of this product. Source attribution for these four nutrients is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-validation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sponge cake and analogous egg-based baked goods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pandan kaya cake exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: sponge cake and egg-based layered cake with sugar filling (GI range 65–70, medium category) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 39.0) / 100 = 26.1, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 26.0 × 1.5 = 39.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (17.1 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (12.0 g/100g) from coconut milk may contribute to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pandan kaya cake to occasional small portions (≤50 g) due to the combined effect of high sugar promoting insulin-driven sodium retention and high saturated fat from coconut milk potentially impairing vascular compliance, even though absolute sodium content (125 mg/100g) is moderate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 17.1 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g ADA threshold, dietary fibre is below 2 g/100g (1.7 g), and GI is 67 (medium), producing a glycemic load of 26.1 per standard 150 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pandan kaya cake is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high free-sugar content combined with low fibre and a medium GI will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation, and the standard 150 g serving delivers a glycemic load of 26.1, well above the recommended per-meal GL ceiling of 10–20 for T2DM management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 12.0 g/100g (predominantly from coconut milk) represents 85% of total fat and substantially exceeds the AHA recommended limit of less than 6% of daily energy from saturated fat; cholesterol is 100 mg/100g and trans fat is present at 0.07 g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of pandan kaya cake; a 150 g serving delivers 18 g of saturated fat, exceeding the AHA daily saturated fat ceiling for most adults, and the combination of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and added sugar collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and promotes endothelial inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 12.0 g/100g from coconut milk far exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold and promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine release; sugar at 17.1 g/100g further amplifies systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid pandan kaya cake due to its high coconut-milk-derived saturated fat and high free-sugar content, both of which are established drivers of pro-inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to RA disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pandan kaya cake is a popular Singaporean dessert cake that combines the fragrant green colour and grassy-sweet aroma of pandan (screwpine) leaves with kaya, a rich coconut jam made from coconut milk, eggs, and sugar. The cake is typically a soft, moist sponge or chiffon-style base infused with pandan extract or juice, layered or filled with kaya spread, and sometimes finished with a light buttercream or additional kaya topping. The texture is tender and slightly springy, with a creamy, sweet filling that carries distinct coconut and egg custard notes. Pandan kaya cake is widely sold in Singaporean bakeries, kopitiam (coffee shop) stalls, and through food delivery platforms, and is commonly consumed as a snack or teatime treat. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 248 kcal, 4.3 g protein, 14.1 g fat (of which 12.0 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 26.0 g carbohydrate including 17.1 g sugar. Dietary fibre is low at 1.7 g/100g. The high saturated fat content from coconut milk and the high free-sugar content make this cake a high-concern food for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, Type 2 Diabetes, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at 67 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 26 per standard 150 g serving, which is clinically significant for blood glucose management. Sodium is moderate at 125 mg/100g. The cake is vegetarian and generally halal-certified in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 for an exact named match. Saturated fat, cholesterol, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally from a delivery-platform source in the input; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for coconut-milk-based sponge cake and are retained as plausible estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sponge and egg-based cake, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pandan kaya cake not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for pandan kaya cake and are not available from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for a sufficiently close analogue as of 2026-04-16. Cholesterol (100 mg/100g), saturated fat (12.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.07 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.33 mg/100g) were originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden delivery-platform source) in the input per_100g_meta; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18133 Cake sponge and coconut cream component entries) and are retained as plausible estimates — source attribution updated to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-validation. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on sponge cake and egg-based layered cake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 65–70); no direct measurement for pandan kaya cake exists in any approved GI source. Glycemic load recalculated for the 150 g default serving: (67 × 39.0) / 100 = 26.1 g. The input record GL of 17.4 appears to reference a smaller reference portion and has been superseded by the 150 g serving calculation. All core macros (protein_g 4.3, fat_g 14.1, carbohydrate_g 26.0) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Foodo; no zero-macro justification is required. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact code exists for pandan kaya cake in either system.
